assumptions: The question concerns hedge accounting in consolidated financial statements.
It is assumed that the recognised intragroup dividend gives rise to an intragroup receivable/payable balance exposed to foreign currency risk.
The facts do not state whether the related foreign exchange gains or losses are fully eliminated on consolidation or affect consolidated profit or loss.
The question is about whether the foreign currency component can be designated, not about the underlying legal validity of the dividend.

approaches.label: General prohibition on intragroup hedged items in consolidation
approaches.reasoning: In consolidated financial statements, hedged items generally must be with parties external to the reporting entity. Intragroup items are therefore not eligible unless a specific exception applies.
approaches.practical_implication: No hedge documentation is permitted in consolidation unless the exposure fits an IFRS 9 exception.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.5 says only items with an external party can be designated as hedged items in consolidated financial statements.

approaches.label: Foreign currency hedge of an intragroup monetary item
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 provides an exception for foreign currency risk on an intragroup monetary item if the resulting exchange differences are not fully eliminated on consolidation. A recognised dividend receivable may fit this route if it is such a monetary item.
conditions: The balance is an intragroup monetary item
The designated risk is foreign currency risk
Foreign exchange gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation
The hedge relationship is formally designated and documented
The hedge meets IFRS 9 effectiveness requirements
approaches.practical_implication: A hedge may be documented in consolidation for the FX exposure of the receivable only if the intragroup monetary-item exception is satisfied.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 allows the foreign currency risk of an intragroup monetary item to qualify in consolidated financial statements when exchange differences are not fully eliminated.
Paragraph 6.4.1 requires formal designation, documentation, identification of the hedged risk and hedge effectiveness assessment.

approaches.label: Designation of the foreign currency risk component
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 allows a component of an item to be designated as the hedged item if the component is separately identifiable and reliably measurable. This supports designating only the FX component, but only for an otherwise eligible hedged item.
conditions: The underlying item is eligible as a hedged item
The foreign currency component is separately identifiable
The foreign currency component is reliably measurable
The hedge documentation identifies the component and hedge ratio
approaches.practical_implication: If the intragroup receivable is eligible under the exception, the hedge can target only its FX component rather than the whole item.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.7 permits designation of a component of an item, including a specific risk component.
Paragraphs B6.3.8-B6.3.11 require the risk component to be separately identifiable and reliably measurable and to satisfy the usual hedge accounting criteria.

approaches.label: Forecast intragroup transaction hedge
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 separately permits highly probable forecast intragroup transactions whose FX risk affects consolidated profit or loss. Once the dividend is already recognised as a receivable, this is no longer the relevant model.
approaches.practical_implication: Do not document the recognised receivable under the forecast-transaction hedge route.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraphs 6.3.6, B6.3.5 and B6.3.6 permit certain forecast intragroup transactions in consolidated financial statements only when the FX risk affects consolidated profit or loss.

approaches.label: Net investment hedge in a foreign operation
approaches.reasoning: IFRIC 16 deals with hedges of foreign currency risk on net investments in foreign operations, where the hedged item is an amount of net assets. That is a different hedge accounting model from an ordinary intragroup dividend receivable.
approaches.practical_implication: Use this model only if the exposure is the group's net investment in a foreign operation, not merely a declared intragroup receivable.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraphs 10-14 describe hedges of net investments based on foreign operations' net assets and group-level hedging instruments.
The Interpretation limits the hedged risk to specified functional-currency relationships and treats the model as applicable only once in the consolidated financial statements.
